text: CHURCH-END, a township in the p. of Shenley, Bucks. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 227.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Tidenham, Gloucestershire. Pop. 253.
CHURCHFIELD. See OUNDLE.
CHURCH-GRESLEY, a parish of Derbyshire, 41 m. SE of Burton-upon-Trent. Area 6,700 acres. Pop. 2,764.
CHURCH-GROVE, a village in Knox co., in the state of Tennessee, U. S., 195 m. E of Nashville.

CHURCH-HILL, a village in Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 182 m. NE of Columbus.—Also a village in Queen Anne's co., Maryland, 48 m. E of Annapolis.—Also a v. in Halifax co., in Virginia, 141 m. SW of Richmond.
CHURCH-HONEYBOURNE-WITH-PODEN. a parish of Worcestershire, 5 m. E of Evesham, and 10 m. from Alcester. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 119.
CHURCH-HULME, a chapelry in the p. of Sandbach, Cheshire, 4 m. E of Middlewich, intersected by the Nantwich and Birmingham railway. Area 760 acres. Pop. 1,008.

CHURCHILL, a parish of Oxfordshire, 23 m. SE of Chipping-Norton. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 651. -Also a parish of Somerset, 4 m. N of Axbridge. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. 970.—Also a parish of Worcestershire, 4 m. ESE of Worcester, 31 m. NE of Kidderminster. Area 950 acres. Pop. 164.—Also a parish 4 m. ESE of Worcester, near the Birmingham and Gloucester railway. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 115. CHURCHILL, MISSISSIPPI, or ENGLISH RIVER, a river of the middle division of the Great central plain of North America, which, rising in Methye lake, under the parallel of 56° N, at an elevation of 1,708 ft. above sea-level, first flows SE through Buffalo lake and La Crosse lake; then, after turning NE, runs E nearly along the parallel of 55° 30', to Nelson lake, whence it pursues a NE course through Granville lake and Bog lake, to Hudson's bay, into which it falls at Fort Churchill. Its entire course probably exceeds 750 m. At the Methye portage it is connected with Lake Athabasca which receives a tributary from Lake Wollaston, while the Stone river, which issues from the same lake, flows into the C. river. This great stream has, in common with the Nelson river of the same district of North America, in the upper part of its course, for many hundred | miles from its origin, the ordinary appearance of a river, being bounded by continuous parallel banks. But on entering the primitive district, they present chains of lake-like dilatations, full of islands, and highly irregular in outline. Many of the numerous arms of these expansions wind for miles through the adjacent country, with scarcely any perceptible current, but connected to each other by one or more straits, in which the stream, obstructed by rocks, and descending from a greater or less elevation, is turbulent and rapid. [Franklin.] The shores of Hudson's bay, at the mouth of the C., are high and rocky, producing only a few small trees and shrubs.
CHURCHILL (CAPE), a promontory on the W coast of Hudson's bay, in N lat. 58° 48', W long. 93° 12' [Raper].

CHURCHILL (FORT), a large fort belonging to the Hudson's Bay company, on a peninsular projection at the mouth of the Churchill river, in N lat. 58° 48′, W long. 94° 5′, opposite Cape Mary. It was taken, in 1782, by the French admiral La Perouse, who spiked the guns and destroyed the factory; and has been suffered to remain in a dilapidated state, though a strong position both by nature and art.
CHURCH-KIRK, a township in the parish of Whalley, Lancashire. Area 620 acres. Pop. 1,545.
CHURCH-KNOWLE, a parish and tything of Dorset, about a mile SW of Corfe Castle, and 5 m. from Wareham. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 463; of tything, 183.
CHURCH-LANGTON, a parish of Leicestershire, 41 m. N of Market-Harborough, on the Welland. Area 4,280 acres. Pop. 869.
CHURCH-LAWFORD, a parish of Warwickshire. Area 2,070 acres. Pop. 333.
CHURCH-LAWTON, a parish of Cheshire, 6 m. from Newcastle. Area-1,540 acres. Pop. 622.
CHURCH-LENCH, a parish of Worcestershire, 6 m. NE of Pershore. Area 2,600 acres. Pop. 393.
CHURCH-OAKLEY, a parish of Hants, 45 m. SSW of Basingstoke, intersected by the Southampton and London railway. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 335.
CHURCH-OVER, a parish of Warwickshire, 41/4 m. NNE of Rugby, intersected by the Midland counties railway. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 339.
CHURCH-PREEN, a parish of Salop. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. 101.
CHURCH-PULVERBATCH, a parish of Salop. Area 4,260 acres. Pop. 543.
CHURCH - SHOCKLACH, a township in the p. of Shocklach, Cheshire. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 178.
CHURCH-SPEEN, a tything in the p. of Speen, Berks. Pop. 224.
CHURCH-STANTON, a parish of Devon, 10 m. NNE of Honiton. Area 4,980 acres. Pop. 1,086.
CHURCH-STOKE, a parish, partly in Salop and Montgomeryshire, 3½ m. SE of Montgomery. Pop. 1,527.
CHURCHSTOW, a parish of Devon, 2 m. NW of Kingsbridge. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 542.
CHURCH-STRETTON. See STRETTON-CHURCH.
CHURCHTOWN, a chapelry in the p. of Whalley, Lancashire, 4 m. E of Blackburn, intersected by the Leeds and Liverpool canal. Area 620 acres. Pop. 1,545.
CHURCH-TOWN, a hamlet in the p. of Backwell, Somerset. Pop. 82.

CHURCHTOWN, a village in the p. of Ballintemple, co. Cork. Pop. 111.—Also a parish in co. Meath, 31 m. SW of Navan. Area 1,336 acres. Pop. 599.—Also a parish in co. Westmeath, 5 m. WSW of Mullingar. Area 5,302 acres. Pop. 1,108. CHU Pop. 158.—Also a parish and v. in co. Cork, 6 m. SSW of Charleville. Area of p. 8,047 acres. Pop. 3,377. Pop. of v. 638.—Also a parish in co. Kildare, on the r. bank of the Barrow. Area 7,330 acres. Pop. 2,294.—Also a v. in the p. of Newcastle, co. Limerick. Pop. 190.
CHURCH-TOWN-QUARTER, a township in the p. of Rockliffe, Cumberland. Pop. 353.

CHURCHVILLE, a village of Riga township, Monroe co., in the state of New York, U. S., 234 m. WNW of Albany, on the Tonawanda railroad. Pop. in 1840, about 300.—Also a village of Clark co., in the state of Missouri, at the junction of the Des Moines with the Mississippi.
CHURCH-WILNE, a liberty in the p. of Wilne, Derbyshire. Pop. 223.
CHURIEBI. See SAO MIGUEL.

CHURILIN, a Tartar village of Russia, in the gov. of Kasan, 49 versts from the city of Kasan, on the road to Arsk. Hills of considerable height begin here, from which streams descend N to the Kasanka.
CHURKULA, a chain of mountains in Tibet, in district of Tufan, in which the Chunak or Heshwuy, the supposed head-stream of the Yang-tse-kiang, takes its rise, in N lat. 33°, long. 15° Wof Peking.
CHURN, or Churnet, a small head-stream of the Thames, rising within 3 m. of Cheltenham, on the E side of the Cotswold hills, and joining the Isis near Cricklade in Wiltshire.
CHURRAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gundwana, 30 m. NE of Chinnoor.
CHURSTON-FERRERS, a parish of Devon, 13 m. W of Brixham, near Tor bay. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. 772.
CHURT, a tything in the p. of Frensham, Surrey. Pop. 423.
CHURTON, a township in the p. of Pulverbatch, Salop. Pop. 90.
CHURTON, or CHERRINGTON, a parish of Wilts, 4 m. NNE of West Lavington. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 428.
CHURTON-BY-ALDFORD, a township in the p. of Aldford, Cheshire. Area 760 acres. Pop. 254.
CHURTON-BY-FARNDON, a township in the p. of Farndon, Cheshire. Area 450 acres. Pop. 132.

CHURWALDEN, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, jurisdiction of Belfort, in a valley of the same name, and on an affluent of the Plessur, at an alt. of 4,232 ft. above sea-level, and 5 m. S of Coire. Pop. 658.

CHURWELL, a township in the p. of Batley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SSW of Leeds, and W of the river Ayre. Area 540 acres. Pop. 1,198. CHUSAN, TCHEUSAN [Macartney], or Chow-shan [Chinese Repository]—which last form is said to be nearest the Chinese pronunciation of the name—an island which gives name to a group of islands on the coast of China, in N lat. 30° 40', E long. 121° 48', 7 m. from the mainland of the prov. of Che-kiang, and 40 m. NE from Ning-po. It derives its name, it is said, from its general resemblance to a boat. Its length is 23 m. from W to E; its breadth from 7 to 11 m.; its circumf. 50 m. It is surrounded by numerous islands or islets, from about one-fourth its own size, to mere barren rocks just rising above the surface of the water. The general appearance of the island is that of a series of undulating hills from 500 to 700 ft. in height, the highest being about 1,100 ft., intersected with broad valleys in the highest state of cultivation, with numerous canals leading in all directions, and towns and villages scattered over it at almost equidistant spots. The land, although hilly, is fertile, and is cultivated with extreme care, in many instances to the very summit of the hills, by spade labour. -Also a hamlet in the p. of Hook, co. Wexford. Even every little bit of low swampy ground on the coast is carefully recovered from the sea where practicable. There are also many beautiful flowering shrubs on the hill-sides, such for example, as the azalea, which here covers many of the hills, and when in flower early in the year, has a gay and imposing appearance. "The flora of C.," says Mr. Fortune, "and all over the mainland in this part of China, is very different from those portions of the south which I have already described. Almost all the species of a tropical character have entirely disappeared, and in their places we find others related to things found in the temperate parts of the world. I here met for the first time the beautiful Glycine Sinensis wild on the hills, where it climbs in hedges and on trees, and allows its flowering branches to hang in graceful festoons by the sides of the narrow roads which lead across the mountains. The Ficus nitida, so common around all the temples and houses in the S, is here unknown, and many of those beautiful flowering genera, which, as I before remarked, are only found on the top of the mountains in Hong-kong, here have chosen less exalted situations. I allude more particularly to the azaleas which abound on the hill-sides of this island. Most persons have seen and admired the azaleas which are yearly brought to the Chiswick fêtes, and which, as individual specimens, surpass, in most instances, those which grow and bloom on their native hills; but few can form any idea of the gorgeous and striking beauty of these azalea-clad mountains, where on every side, as far as our vision extends, the eye rests on masses of flowers of dazzling brightness and surpassing beauty. Nor is it azaleas alone which meet the eye and claim our attention; clematises, wild roses, honeysuckles, the Glycine Sinensis, noticed above, and a hundred other things, mingle their flowers with them, and make us confess that, after all, China is indeed 'the central flowery land.' There are several species of myrtaceous and other ericaceous plants, which are also common on the hills, but no species of heath has been ever found; and I believe the genus does not exist in this part of China." The camphor and tallow tree abound in the valleys; and whole forests of different varieties of bamboo give a kind of tropical character to the general scenery. The tea plant (Thea viridis) is also cultivated in small patches on the hill-sides; but with the exception of a little which is sent to Ning-po, little or none is manufactured for exportation. The poor people, or small farmers, generally dry the leaves in the sun, for their own use. "Indeed," says Mr. Fortune, "it is questionable if it would pay, as the soil is scarcely rich enough; and, although the shrub grows pretty well, it is far from being so luxuriant as it is in the larger tea-districts of the mainland, which I afterwards visited." Rice is principally grown in the low grounds, and sweet potatoes on the hills; but on the tops of the latter, and on some parts a considerable way down, everything is in a state of nature, the long grass and shrubs being periodically cut down for burning. In winter, after the second crop of rice is gathered, the ground is immediately ploughed up, and green crops, such as cabbage, beans, pease, &c., are planted and sown. A considerable quantity of cotton and hemp is also cultivated,-the latter producing a strong fibre, which is peeled and prepared by the natives. The small ox-plough, and the waterwheel-which is here wrought by hand-are the two principal implements in husbandry. The plough is a rude thing, but it answers the purpose remarkably well, and is probably better for the Chinese in their present state, and with their small oxen and buffaloes, than a heavier and improved implement might be. An immense quantity of water is raised simply and easily by the water-wheel, and is made to flow into the different rice-flats with great rapidity.

CHUSAN. [Athenœum.] There are usually two crops of rice, and one of oil-seed, raised annually on this island from a cultivated surface of about 34,000 acres. The gardens are most prolific of all kinds of vegetables, of which the growth is stimulated here, as in other parts of China, by liquid manure. Good water abounds not only at the capital, but throughout the island. Beef, pork, poultry, and game, with bread of English quality, and English vegetables and fruit, were abundantly supplied our troops when we held C., at one-half of the sum they cost at Hong-kong. The island is intersected with roads-not intended for wheeled conveyances-from 5 to 7 ft. broad, and paved and flagged throughout; and it is so thickly studded with villages, that those who have the best opportunity of forming an opinion on the subject, believe that 100 well-populated towns and superior villages would be found on it, containing from 1,000 to 5,000 inhabitants. The extensive gardens, the cultivated hills, and the rice plains, with numerous rivulets, and the crowds of birds to be observed in the trees, impart to the whole the character of the richest landscape and enhance the pleasures of its active and industrious pop, who are estimated at 270,000. Captain Hall characterises them as being generally a hardy and independent race of people, and states that up to the close of the war in which we were lately engaged with China, "it never could be said that we really had possession of more than the actual city within the walls of Ting-hai, and its suburbs." [Voy. of the Nemesis.] C. is the Montpelier of China. Woollen dresses are worn here for nine months of the year. Ice is frequently formed in quantities, and lasts until the month of September. There are but three months which can be called hot, -June, July, and August. In this latter month the therm. stands, on an average, at 83°, but sinks at night to 73°. The next month it subsides to 74° in the day. Then comes winter, with hoar-frost, and ice half-an-inch thick. In January and February the therm. often stands at 20°. In March it rises to 28°, but the hills continue to be capped with snow, and the cheerful fire is kept up till June. The commerce of this island is considerable. Ships can reach it during the NE monsoon with equal ease as they can Hong-kong, although it is 700 m. to the N. Fishing is universal around the island, and it is calculated that not fewer than 70,000 vessels come from the continent, and remain for three months off C. employed in fishing. They are attended by boats filled with ice, in which the fish are packed, and then despatched to the neighbouring coast.—The principal town in the island is Ting-hai or Ting-hae: whence the island is sometimes called by the natives Ting-hae-hyen. It is surrounded by walls 30 ft. high, and 2 m. in extent, along which, at the distance of 300 yards, are square stone towers. Ting-hai bears some resemblance to Venice, but, of course, on a small scale. It is in some degree surrounded, as well as intersected by canals. The streets, which are no more than alleys or narrow passages, are paved with square flat stones; but the houses, unlike the Venetian buildings, are low, and mostly of one story. The town is full of shops, containing chiefly articles of clothing, food, and furniture, displayed to full advantage. Throughout the place there is an appearance of quick and active industry, beyond the natural effect of a clime not quite 30° from the equator. The streets are narrow, being mere lanes; but when the British troops entered this place in September, 1840, many of the public buildings, particularly its principal temple dedicated to the worship of Fu, excited great astonishment among those who fancied they were in a half-barbarous country.-C. has two principal bays, Ting-hai on the S and Sing-kong on the W coast. The former is capable of containing 100 sail of square-rigged vessels. There are also other good ports. In Chusan or Ting-hai harbour, in N lat. 30° 36', E long. 121° 41', the ordinary rise of the tide is 12 ft. 6 in.; the depth of the water from 4 to 10 fath. This harbour, which is formed by three small islands, affords several excellent sites for docks and dock-yards, so much wanting in the Chinese seas. Sing-kong likewise seems to be especially adapted for the purpose on account of its excellent anchorage. It is completely land-locked; but the great rapidity of the currents or tides, the rise of all of which varies from 6 to 12 ft., and the narrowness of the passages render this harbour difficult to approach. By the stipulations of our final treaty with China, the possession of this island was restored to that country in December 1845, or rather within three months afterwards. "If C. were still in our possession." says Mr. M. Martin, "we would be within two days' sail of Japan, with its 30,000,000 of highly civilized inhabitants; and more easily regain our lost position in that rich empire. Our occupation of C. would sooner or later open to us an entire new commercial world. We would also have as our neighbour the wealthy and extensive kingdom of Corea; likewise Mantshuria, Loo-Choo, the Kurile islands, and other regions around. Constant war with China is inevitable, unless we are permanently established at C., from whence a single warsteamer could in a few hours stop the whole internal navigation of the empire. The imperial grain ships (6,000) must pass it on their way to Peking; their cargoes are valued at 24,000,000 taels (£5,000,000 sterling). It requires no great foresight to predict whether this would not be more efficacious than blowing up a few mat-forts or sending our brave soldiers from Hong-kong, which is at the extremity of a line of sea-coast of 2,000 m." The East India company had a factory on C. till the middle of the 18th cent.

CHUSAN ISLANDS, or Chusan Archipelago, a numerous group of islands off the E coast of China, and administratively attached to the prov. of Che-kiang, between the parallels of 29° 40′ and 30° 20', until recently little known to European navigators. They are scattered around the principal island which gives its name to the group, and so thickly that, according to Staunton, in a space of about 60 m. in length, and 30 m. in breadth, to the S of the large island, their number exceeds 300, while those to the N are apparently not less numerous. Many of them, however, are mere barren rocks, and some of them are visited only occasionally for the sake of a patch of cultivation which may find place upon them; but others teem with an industrious population, who form enterprising fishermen and small merchants. Their general aspect is that of a hill rising from the sea with a regular slope, and terminating in one or more flat round tops. To the N of Chusan, the larger islands are Tae-shan, Chang-tow, and Keu-shan. Between these and the N coast of Chusan lie Chang-pih, and Lan-sew. To the E of Chusan lies Pu-tu, which is described as inhabited only by bonzes or priests, and as covered with small temples and picturesque grottoes: to the W is Tsih-tze or Blackwall island. Immediately S of Chusan are the small islands of Tamaou or Tower hill, Teijo or Elephant island, Seaou-Keu or Deer island, Ao-shan, Pih-lou, Ta-kan and Kin-ho, and North Chuckea. To the S of these is the large island of Tygosan, which is separated by a channel half-a-mile in breadth from a peninsular projection of the mainland, which, in Ketow North point, 7 m. E of Tygosan, approaches within 5 m. of Tsing-luy point, on the S coast of Chusan. On the same | near the l. bank of the Cogollos. CIA parallel with the peninsular projection of the mainland lie Tang-fou island, and South Chukea. To the S lie Mei-shan or Plum island, Fu-to, the large island of Luh-wang, Beak island, Vernon island, and Taou-hwa. The tides and currents are rapid among these islands; and the sudden overfalls require a careful and well-informed navigation; but a good survey of the principal portion of the C. archipelago was executed by Commander Collinson in 1844. The Amherst, in the course of her experimental voyage to the NE coast of China, in 1832, visited the C. group, and landed at Ting-hae, where the expedition was well received.
CHUSCLAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Bagnols. Pop. 782. The environs afford good wine.
CHUSHMEH. See CHESHMEH.

CHUSMA-I-SHADI, a halting-place in Afghanistan, on the route from Candahar to Ghuzni, 101 m. from Shuftul, and 181 m. from Gojan. Alt. 6,668 ft. above sea-level. Major Hough says, about 4 m. from this place there is a plain on which 50,000 men might encamp, with a good supply of running water and abundance of grass.
CHUT, a ruined city of N India, in Sirhind, in N lat. 30° 37′, 18 m. E by S of Sirhind. It is now entirely gone to ruins, but presents a few houses amidst pleasant gardens and groves.
CHUTAKH, a village of Turkey, in Bulgaria, 33 hours N of Casan, containing about 200 houses.
CHUTANAGPUR. See CHOTA-NAGPORE.
CHUTE, a parish of Wilts, 31 m. NE of Ludgershall. Area 3,220 acres. Pop. 525.
CHUTE-FOREST, an extra-parochial district of Wilts, 5 m. NNE of Luagershall. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 135.
CHUTTERBAI, a fortress in Afghanistan, on the r. bank of the Indus, in N lat. 34° 20', which is held by an independent chief.
CHUTTERPUR. See CHATTERPUR.
CHUWAL. See CHOWAL.
CHUXULU, a town of Yucatan, on the N coast, 28 m. N of Merida.
CHUY, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Che-keang, division of Shaou-hing-fu.
CHUYES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Pelussin, 19 m. E of Saint- Etienne. Pop. 1,284. The environs produce good wine.
CHVEKSTNAOU, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Vilna, district and 63 m. WNW of Rossieny, and 15 m. from the Baltic.

CHWANG-LANG-TING, or Tcheang-lang-ing, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kan-su, division and 90 m. SSE of Leang-chu-fu. The town is in N lat. 36° 47', on an affluent of the Hoang-ho, and near the Great wall.
CHYNPUR-BARI, or BARRI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, district of Raissin, 48 m. ESE of Bhopal, on an affluent of the Nerbudda.
CHYPIS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, dizain and 13 m. S of Sider, at the confluence of the Navigenza with the Rhone. It contains a saline spring.
CHYROW, a town of Galicia, in the gov. and 60 m. WSW of Lemberg, circle and 15 m. W of Sambor, on the l. bank of the Strwiaz. It possesses manufactories of hosiery.
CHYTRIANA, a small island in the Grecian archipelago, to the S of the island of Siphanto.
CIABLESE. See CHABLAIS.
CIADONCHA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, prov. and 17 m. SW of Burgos, partido of Lerma,
CIAMPA. See SIAMPA.

CIAMUT, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, at the head of the valley of Tavetch, 21 leagues up the mountains from Dissentis, at an alt. of nearly 5,000 ft. above sea-level. Rye is the only grain cultivated at this height.
CIANCIANA, or SAN ANTONIO, a town of Sicily, in the district and 6 m. S of Bivona. Pop. 3,400. Nearly 8,000 cantars of sulphur are annually extracted from mines in the vicinity.

CIBAO, a mountain-range in the island of Hayti, near its centre, forming apparently the nucleus of the mountain-system of that island. It has an elevation of between 8,000 and 9,000 ft. The Neybe, the Artibonite, the Grand Yaque, and the Yonna, the principal rivers of the island, have their sources on its sides. Rich gold mines were at one time wrought upon the C.

CIBIN, or ZIBIN, a river of Transylvania, an affluent of the Aluta. It rises on the Fromaberg, and flows first NE to Hermanstadt, where it bends round to the SE, and joins the Aluta on the r. bank, opposite Porcsed, after a course of about 50 m.

CIBOLO, a river of Texas, which rises in a peak of the Guadalupe mountains, and flowing S by E, joins the San Antonio on the l. bank, after a course of 150 m. It is a comparatively small stream, destitute of tributaries.
CIBOURE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Saint-Jean-de- Luz. Pop. 2,153.
CIBRIAN - DE - CASTRO, or CEBRIAN - DE - CASTROTORAFE (SAN), a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and partido and 14 m. NNE of Zamora, between the Esla and Duero.

CIBRIAN-DE-MAZOTE, or CEBRIAN-DE-MAZOTE (SAN), a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 17 m. W of Valladolid, partido of Motadel-Marqués. CICACOLE, CHICACOLE, or SIKABOL, an ancient division of the Northern Circars, in Hindostan, forming the larger portion of the newly created collectorate of Ganjam, and comprising the towns of C., Kalingapatam, Bimlipatam, and Vizagapatam.—The town of C. is in N lat. 18° 15', E long. 84°, on the N bank of a river of the same name, which descends from the mountain-frontier of Gundwana, and flows into the sea 3 m. below the town.
CICAGNA, a village of Sardinia, division of Genoa, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 10 m. NW of Chiavari, at the foot of the Apennines.
CICALA, or CIGALA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 11 m. NE of Nieastro, and 12 m. NNW of Catanzaro. Pop. 1,660. It suffered considerably from an earthquake in 1783.

CICCIANO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 3 m. N of Nola. Pop. 3,060. It contains several churches, and is an ancient fief of the knights of the order of Malta.
CICERALE, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato-Citra, district and 11 m. NW of Il Vallo, and 33 m. SE of Salerno. Pop. 1,100.

CICERO, a township of Onondaga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 140 m. WNW of Albany, bounded on the NE by Oneida lake, and intersected by Chittenango creek. It has a level surface, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 2,464; of village about 200 .- Also a town of Hamilton co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 389.
CICILIANO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the comarca and 26 m. ENE of Rome, and 8 m. E of Tivoli.
CICOBASSO, a river of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Assuay and prov. of Loza, which de- ~ CIE scends from the Andes, and unites with the Zamora, an affluent of the Santiago.
CICOLA, a river of Dalmatia, which runs WNW; passes Dernis; and unites with the Kerka, 5 m. W of Scardona.

CIDACOS, a river of Spain, in Old Castile, which takes its rise in the Sierra Cebollera; runs NE; passes Arnedo and Calahorra; and falls into the Ebro a little below the latter town, after a total course of 42 m. CIDADE. For some compound names beginning with Cidadea Portuguese word, signifying 'city'-see the latter name. Thus, for CIDADE-DO-RECIFE, see RECIFE.
CIDROINE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Joigny. Pop. 874.

CIECHANOW, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Plock, obwod and 15 m. SE of Przasnysz, situated in a marshy district on the l. bank of the Lidinia. Pop. 2,400, chiefly Jews. Its principal manufacture is that of leather.

CIECHANOWIEC, a town situated partly on the I. bank of the Nurzek, in the Russian prov. of Bialystock, 30 m. WSW of Bielsk; and partly on the opposite side of the river in the Polish gov. of Augustow, obwod and 40 m. SE of Lomzs. The pop. of the Russian part of the town is 2,700, of whom onehalf are Jews; of the Polish 567.

CIEGAS (Bocas), or Boquillas Cerradas, four openings communicating betwixt the gulf of Mexico and the Madre lagune, on the coast of Tamaulipas, in N lat. 25°, W long. 97° 45'. They all occur within a league's distance.
CIEGO (EL), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Alava, partido of La Guardia, on the l. bank of the Ebro, 9 m. WNW of Logrono. Pop. 980.

CIEMPOZUELOS, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 20 m. S of Madrid, partido of Getafe, on a hill near the r. bank of the Jarama. Pop. 2,044. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a parish-church, several convents, and a custom-house. It has a manufactory of saltpetre, and in the vicinity are the salt-works of Espartinas.

CIENFUEGOS, or FERNANDINA, a town of the island of Cuba, cap. of the jurisdiction of the same name, in the Departemento-del-Centro. Pop. in 1841, 2,437, of whom 1,494 were whites, 448 free coloured, and 495 slaves. Sugar is extensively cultivated in the environs.
CIEPIELOW, a small town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, obwod of Opatow, 23 m. ESE of Radom, on the r. bank of the Ilza.
CIEPLICE, a village of Galicia, in the circle and 30 m. NNW of Przemysl. Pop. 2,125.

CIER-DE-LUCHON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Bagnères-de- Luchon, at the entrance of the Val-de-Luchon, 21 m. SSW of Saint-Gaudens. Pop. 475. It contains quarries of fine red marble.
CIER-DE-RIVIE'RE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Saint-Bertrand, 6 m. SW of Saint-Gaudens. Pop. 789. It contains quarries of grey marble.
CIERGES (SAINT), a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district and 4 m. WNW of Moudon, and 14 m. NNE of Lausanne. Pop. 444.
CIERGUE (SAINT), a village of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. and 5 m. WNW of Langres. It contains a paper-mill.
CIERP, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Saint-Béat. Pop. 1,011.
CIERS. See LES AVENCE'RES.
CIERS-CHAMPAGNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inférieure, cant. of Archiac. Pop. 1,007.

CIERS-LA-LANDE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, arrond. | of Blaye. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,138; in 1841, 13,550. The town is 13 m. N of Blaye. Pop. 2,707.
CIERS-DU-TAILLON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Mirambeau. Pop. 1,378.
CIERVA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 18 m. E of Cuenca, partido of Canete-y-la- Huergina. The environs contain extensive quarries of marble.
CIES ISLANDS. See BAYONA.
CIESZANOW, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 45 m. W of Zolkiew, and 8 m. N of Lubaczow, near the r. bank of the Tanew.
CIESZKOWICE, a town of Galicia, in the prov. of Lemberg, circle of Sandec, 21 m. NE of Nowy- Sandec, on the r. bank of the Biala. Pop. 1,910. It has some linen manufactories.
CIEUTAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Pyrénées, cant. of Bagnères-en-Bigorre. Pop. 1,246.
CIEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Nantiat, 10 m. S of Bellac, on a little lake on the S side of the Montagnes-de- Blond. Pop. 1,728. A fair is held monthly.
CIEZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. of Donzy. Pop. 1,263.

CIEZA, or Zieza, a town of Spain, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 25 m. NW of Murcia, on the l. bank of the Segura. Pop. 6,917. It is of great antiquity. It has some manufactories of linen. The partido comprises 9 pueblos.—Also a river of Leon, in the prov. of Palencia, which runs S, and unites with the Carrion near Amusco.
CIFALA, a village of Sicily, in the prov. and 20 m. SSE of Palermo, district and 17 m. SW of Termini. It contains some thermal springs.

CIFUENTES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 36 m. E of Guadalajara. Pop. 1,465. It is of recent origin, and contains a parish-church, a custom-house, and sevveral convents. The partido comprises 52 pueblos.

CIGALES, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 7 m. N of Valladolid, partido of Valoria, near the r. bank of the Pisuerga. Pop. 1,933. It contains a parish-church, a bank, and a custom-house.

CIGLIANO, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 22 m. W of Vercilli, and 26 m. NE of Turin. Pop. 3,600. A fair is held twice a-year. Rice is extensively cultivated in the environs.
CIGNE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. and 9 m. N of Mayenne, cant. of Ambrières. Pop. 1,474.
CIGOGNE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, cant. of Saint-Benin-d'Azy, 9 m. ENE of Nevers. Extensive iron-works have been recently established here.
CILAVEGNA, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Lomellina, 2 m. NE of Mortara. Pop. 3,600.
CILCEN, or KILKEN, a parish in Flintshire, in the vale of Clwyd, 4 m. WNW of Mold. Pop. 1,267.
CILCENIN, or KILKENIN, a parish in Cardiganshire, on the Teifi river, 9 m. E by S of Cardigan. Pop. 647.
CILLANUEVA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 9 m. S of Leon, on an affluent of the Esla.

CILLA-PERLATA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 33 m. WSW of Vittoria, on the r. bank of the Ebro. CILLI, CILLEY, or ZILLI, a circle of Austria, comprising the S part of Lower Styria; bounded on the NW by the Husva range, which separates it from Il- CIN 566 lyria; on the NE by the Bach range, which separates it from the Styrian circle of Marburg; on the E by Croatia; on the S by the course of the Save from Rann to Sagorie, and by the Stein Alps, which separate it from Illyria. It is a very mountainous district. Its principal rivers are the Sann, flowing S to the Save through what has been called the Styrian Switzerland; the Schale, flowing N to the Drave; and the Dran, flowing E to the Drave. Area 65 German or 1,380 English sq. m. Pop. in 1816, 162,288; in 1837, 208,110. The valuation for the land-tax in 1837 was 4,245,649 florins = £424,564. The principal towns in the circle are the capital, of the same name, Windischgratz, Windisch-Feistritz, and Rann. -The town of C. is situated on the Sann, at the embouchure of the Koding, 60 m. S by W of Grätz. Pop. 1,660. It has some trade in grain and wine, and the mineral water of Rokitsch. The great railway of Central Europe, designed to connect Hamburg with Trieste, by way of Vienna, passes C., and was opened from C. to Laibach on the 17th Sept., 1849.— C. was the Claudia Celeia of the Romans. In 1798 it was nearly destroyed by the explosion of a powdermagazine.
CILLEROS, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 48 m. NNW of Caceres, partido of Hoyos. Pop. 1,506.
CILLERUELO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. NE of Segovia, partido of Riaza, near the l. bank of the Caravias.
CILLERUELO-DE-ABAXO, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 27 m. S of Burgos, partido and 7 m. SSW of Lerma.
CILLERUELO-DE-ARRIBA, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Burgos, partido and 9 m. SE of Lerma, near the r. bank of the Esgueva.
CILMARGH, a hamlet in the parish of Llandeveylog, in Carmarthenshire, 3 m. from Kidwelly. Pop. 160.
CIL-RHEDDYN. See KIL-RHEDDYN.

CIMARRON, or CIMERONE, a large river rising in the NW extremity of Texas, under the parallel of 37° 30', near the Spanish - peaks, and flowing first ESE, and then ENE to the Arkansas, which it joins on the r. bank, in about 100° 15' E long. Between the C. and the Arkansas, on the N frontier of Texas, extends what has been called "a prairie ocean," a vast monotonous plain in which over a distance of 40 m. all is level as the sea, and the compass forms the only safe guide of the traveller.

CIMBRISHAMM, a town of Sweden, in the laen and 30 m. SSE of Christianstad, in the hærrad of Gerresta, on the Baltic coast, in N lat. 55° 33' 27". Pop. in 1805, 677; in 1833, 1,032. It has a small active port, from which the chief exports are timber, fish, and grain.
CIMBRO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 30 m. NW of Milan. Pop. 250.
CIMINNA, or CIMNA, a town of Sicily, in the district and 12 m. SW of Termini. Pop. 6,150.
CIMITILE, a town of Naples, in the Terra-di- Lavoro, 1 m. N of Nola. Pop. 2,296.
CIMOLI. See KIMOLI.

CIMONE (MONTE), a mountain of Italy, in the duchy of Modena, in N lat. 44° 11', 6 m. E of Pieve- Pelago, forming the culminating point in the Central Apennines. Alt. 1,166 toises or 7,452 ft. [Dict. Geog. Univ. Paris 1829]; 7,071 ft. [Guibert]; 6,975 ft. [Penny Cyclop. and Balbi]. It is chiefly composed of greywacke.
CINALOA. See SINALOA.

CINCA, a river of Spain, rising in the Pyrenees, in the prov. of Huesca, 18 m. NW of Venasque; passing Puertolas, Ainsa, Barbastro, Monzon, and Fraga; and uniting with the Segre, 1 m. above the confluence of that river with the Ebro, after a course of 100 m. from N to S. It receives the Ara, on the r. bank, at Ainsa; the Vero, on the r., at Barbastro; the Sosa, on the l., at Monzon; and the Alcanadre, near Vallabor.

CINCINNATI, a city, port-of-entry, and the capital of Hamilton co. in the SW of the state of Ohio, U. S., 116 m. SW of Columbus, 497 m. W by N of Washington; in 39° 5′ 54" N lat., 84° 27' W long. from Greenwich [American Alm.]. It is situated on the N bank of the Ohio, 455 m. below Pittsburg, and 1,548 m. above New Orleans, on an elevated plain 540 ft. above tide-water at Albany, and 25 ft. below the level of Lake Erie; but the plain is divided into two small table-lands, one of which is from 40 to 60 ft. higher than the other. The river is 600 yards wide at C., and is navigable for steam-vessels as far as Pittsburg. The shore at the landing-place being paved to low-water mark—which is 133 ft. below the level of Lake Erie - and supplied with floating wharves adapted to the rise and fall of the river, forms a safe landing for boats at all seasons of the year. The descent from the upper plain of C. to lowwater mark on the Ohio averages 180 ft. The site occupied by the city is near the E extremity of a valley about 12 m. in circumf., which is skirted on the N by a ridge of picturesque and finely wooded hills rising to the height of 300 ft. above the plain. The river divides this rich and well-cultivated valley into two parts: on the N side is C.; and opposite to it the two small manufacturing towns of Newport and Covington, separated from each other by the river Lecking, which flows into the Ohio. The aspect of the city from the hills is beautiful; but as approached by water, it is neither extensive nor commanding. Excepting on the margin of the river, it is regularly laid out in streets and alleys, crossing each other at right angles. The streets running E and W are denominated, proceeding from the river, First, Second, &c.; those running N and S are named after the native trees, as Walnut, Sycamore, &c. Many of the streets are well-paved and planted with trees. The houses are generally of red or party-coloured brick. Most of them have a small frontage filled with flowering shrubs, of which the Althea frutex is the most abundant [Marryat]. The city is supplied with water raised from the Ohio river by steam-power. The central portion of the city contains spacious warehouses and stores, with handsome dwelling-houses; but the extensive plain, in its outer parts, is only partially built upon. The climate, though variable and subject to extremes of heat and cold, is considered healthy. In 1795, C. numbered only 500 inhabitants; in 1800, 750; in 1810, 2,540; in 1820, 9,642; in 1830, 24,831; in 1840, 46,338. This pop. is composed of emigrants from all the states of the Union, and almost every country of Europe. The Dutch and Germans are especially numerous. Besides the settled, there is estimated to be a floating population of from 2,000 to 3,000 always present in C. Of the pop. in 1840, there were engaged in commerce, 2,226; in manufactures and trade, 10,866; in learned professions, 434. C. is the seat of numerous manufacturing establishments, among which are cotton and woollen mills, flour - mills, saw - mills, distilleries, breweries, lead works, iron works, and steam-boat building-yards. It is the largest pork-market in the union. Almost all the hogs fed in the oak-forests of Ohio, Kentucky, and Western Virginia, are driven to C. to be slaughtered and cured. By means of canals, railroads, and macadamized turnpikes, the internal trade of C. is very extensive. The Miami canal extends from C. to the Wabash and Erie canal; and the Whitewater canal extends to Cambridge. The tonnage of the l $ CIN port in 1840, was 12,052. There were 7 daily papers issued in C. in 1840, besides tri-weekly and weekly papers; and several miscellaneous as well as religious magazines are here published monthly.-The municipal government consists of a president, recorder, and 21 councillors, -3 for each of the 7 wards into which the city is divided. The college of C., founded in 1819, has 8 professors. Woodward college, founded in 1831, has 5 professors. St. Xavier Roman Catholic college, founded in 1840, has 14 professors, and was attended by 330 students in 1848. There are also a medical college, and a law school; and in 1821 a college of teachers was established here for promoting the interests of schools in the Mississippi valley. The charitable institutions of the city are numerous and respectable. There are 40 churches, a court-house, 4 markets, a U. S. Land office, a theatre, and a museum.—C. was founded in 1789, by emigrants from New England and New Jersey. Its site in 1780 was an unbroken forest; but a few years after, with a view to the Indian war, a post, called Fort Washington, was erected upon it. In 1819 it was chartered as a city; and it now ranks as the sixth place in point of pop. in the United States; and, being the great emporium of the West, its importance must increase with the growth of the rapidly rising country to which it belongs.
CINCINNATI, a village in Ralls co., in the state of Missouri, 101 m. NNE of Jefferson, on the Salt river.
CINCINNATUS, a township in Courtland co., in the state of New York, U. S., 12 m. SE of Homer, intersected by Ostelic creek and its tributaries. Pop. 1,301.
CINCOS, a town of Peru, in the dep. of Lima, 62 m. ESE of Lima.—Also a town of Peru, in the dep. of Junin, 105 m. E of Lima, 8 m. S of Iconja.
CINCUDENTI, an extensive forest-tract in the island of Sardinia, in the commune of Agius, NW of Tempio. It is principally composed of oak, ilex, and cork, with an underwood of arbutus and lentiscus.

CINEY, or CHINEY, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. and 9 m. ENE of Dinant, on the Haljoux, in the fertile district called Condroz celebrated for its pasturage. Its manufactures consist principally in pottery and agricultural implements; it has also some trade in corn, wool, cattle, and wood. Pop. in 1835, 1,435.—In 1276 this town was reduced to ashes in 'the war of the Cow,' as it was called. It was taken in 1667 by Louis XIV.
CINGOLI, or CINGOLO, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 12 m. WNW of Macerata, on the r. bank of the Musone. Pop. 2,300.
CINIGIANO, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 20 m. NE of Grosseto. Pop. 467.
CINISELLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 6 m. N of Milan. Pop. 2,300.
CINISI, a town of Sicily, in the district and 15 m. WNW of Palermo, near the coast. Pop. 3,459.
CINQ-MARS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Langeais, 9 m. WSW of Tours. Pop. 1,626.
